attack_summary: An active ICMP flood (network DoS) was launched from a compromised Ubuntu host (UbuntuComputer, 192.168.1.3) reaching external target 7.0.14.254 using hping3; the agent maintained contact to a remote Caldera server (203.0.113.1) and the attacker remained present in-network.

mitigations.name: Insert LAN-side inline Snort IPS
mitigations.brief_description: Removed the direct FortiGate <-> switch link, added a preconfigured Snort IPS (snort-ids-1) and reconnected topology so traffic flows FortiGate -> Snort -> Cisco switch. Snort, running inline in IPS mode, detected and blocked malicious high-rate ICMP flows in real time while leaving the compromised host on the network.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

mitigations.name: FortiGate-based micro-segmentation / egress restriction for compromised host
mitigations.brief_description: On FortiGate7.0.14-1 created address objects for the Ubuntu host and Caldera server, added custom services, and applied firewall policies that (a) deny ICMP (PING) from the Ubuntu host, (b) allow only TCP/8443 to the Caldera server, and (c) allow DNS (UDP/53). Logging was enabled to monitor traffic. This constrained the host's ability to launch high-volume ICMP floods while keeping it on-network.

key_observations: Both mitigations complied with the live-response constraint: the compromised host remained connected and the attacker stayed present while the attack was stopped.
Attack success rate dropped from 1.0 to 0.0 after the mitigations; the ICMP flood was effectively halted.

Collateral effect: general outbound Internet HTTP connectivity (e.g., to 1.1.1.1) was blocked after changes—expected due to strict egress/inline blocking and requiring verification/tuning to restore legitimate flows.

A minor FortiGate configuration parse error occurred when creating the DNS custom service; the policy referencing it was present, but the DNS service definition should be verified to ensure DNS is truly allowed.
Interface name mapping (port1/port2) should be confirmed to ensure firewall policies apply in the intended direction; topology operations were otherwise correct and reversible.
Using a preconfigured inline IPS (Snort) provided immediate automated blocking without per-device signature tuning; combining network-level IPS with firewall egress controls gave layered protection.
overall_conclusion: The ICMP flood was successfully mitigated using two complementary, non-destructive mitigations: inserting a LAN-side inline Snort IPS to block malicious high-rate ICMP flows, and enforcing FortiGate egress/micro-segmentation policies to deny ICMP from the compromised host and permit only minimal management traffic. Both actions respected the constraint of keeping the compromised host in-network and reduced attack success to zero. Follow-up actions: verify DNS service correctness, confirm FortiGate interface mappings, review Snort alerts/logs to understand blocking reasons, and tune policies to restore any required legitimate outbound services while preserving protection.
